title: Psalms 142
subtitle: I Cry Aloud to the LORD

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “No One Is Concerned for Me”

heb.paragraph: A maskil of David, when he was in the cave (məʿārâ). David cries aloud (v.1), pours out his complaint (v.2). "When my spirit grows faint, you know my way" (v.3). Snares are hidden in his path (v.3). "Look and see, there is no one at my right hand; no one is concerned for me. I have no refuge; no one cares for my life" (v.4). The loneliest verse in the Psalter: no one at the right hand, no concern, no refuge, no care. Total isolation.

notes.note: "I cry aloud to the LORD; I pour out before him my complaint." The cave prayer: raw, loud, unfiltered. The cave provides no audience but God.

"When my spirit grows faint, you know my way. No one is concerned for me; no one cares for my life." The extremity of isolation: not attacked but IGNORED. No enemy needs to strike when no friend remains.

refs.note: David hides in the cave of Adullam, gathering outcasts and debtors.
Jesus in Gethsemane: 'My soul is overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death.'
Paul despaired of life itself, relying on God who raises the dead.

hist.context: Psalm 142's superscription places David in 'the cave'—either Adullam (1 Sam 22) or En Gedi (1 Sam 24). This maskil (instructive psalm) pours out complaint with raw honesty: 'I cry aloud to the LORD; I lift up my voice for mercy.' The cave setting amplifies the sense of being trapped, pursued, and abandoned. Yet David addresses God as 'my portion in the land of the living.'

sections.header: Verses 5–7 — “Set Me Free from My Prison”

heb.paragraph: "Set me free from my prison (masger), that I may praise your name" (v.7). The cave is a prison. David cannot praise while confined. The request: freedom FOR praise. "Then the righteous will gather about me because of your goodness to me" (v.7). Liberation produces community: the isolated man surrounded by the righteous. The psalm moves from total loneliness (v.4) to anticipated community (v.7). The cave psalm: confined → freed → surrounded.

notes.note: "I cry to you, LORD; I say, ‘You are my refuge, my portion in the land of the living.’" In the cave, God is the only portion. The ḥēleq theology of Psalms 16 and 73: God himself is enough.

"Set me free from my prison, that I may praise your name. Then the righteous will gather about me." Freedom’s purpose: praise. Freedom’s result: community. The isolated cave-dweller becomes the centre of a righteous gathering.

refs.note: Remember those in prison as if you were together with them.
Paul and Silas pray and sing in prison; God breaks their chains.
The LORD sets prisoners free—the promise undergirding David's plea.

hist.context: David's complaint intensifies: 'No one is concerned for me... no one cares for my life.' This is existential isolation. The plea to be freed from prison may be literal (the cave as prison) or metaphorical (trapped circumstances). The promise to praise 'when you have dealt bountifully with me' anticipates deliverance. The righteous gathering around is the restored community—David's vindication becoming communal celebration.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: The cave provides no audience but God…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVWhen my spirit grows faint within me, it is you who watch over my way. In the path where I walk people have hidden a snare for me.ESVWhen my spirit faints within me, you know my way! In the path where I walk they have hidden a trap for me.NIVI cry aloud to the Lord; I lift up my voice to the Lord for mercy.ESVWith my voice I cry out to the LORD; with my voice I plead for mercy to the LORD.

rec.text: ""I cry aloud to the LORD; I pour out before him my complaint." The cave prayer: raw, loud, unfiltered. The cave provides no audience but God."

rows.label: The Cave — 142:1–4
rows.text: "No one is concerned for me; no one cares." The loneliest verse. Total isolation. The cave as prison.

hebtext.note: A maskil of David, when he was in the cave (məʿārâ). David cries aloud (v.1), pours out his complaint (v.2). "When my spirit grows faint, you know my way" (v.3). Snares are hidden in his path (v.3). "Look and see, there is no one at my right hand; no one is concerned for me. I have no refuge; no one cares for my life" (v.4). The loneliest verse in the Psalter: no one at the right hand, no concern, no refuge, no care. Total isolation.

"Set me free from my prison (masger), that I may praise your name" (v.7). The cave is a prison. David cannot praise while confined. The request: freedom FOR praise. "Then the righteous will gather about me because of your goodness to me" (v.7). Liberation produces community: the isolated man surrounded by the righteous. The psalm moves from total loneliness (v.4) to anticipated community (v.7). The cave psalm: confined → freed → surrounded.

themes.note: Faith peaks: praising from the cave. Worship: freedom’s purpose is praise. The loneliest psalm resolves in anticipated community.

prayer_prompt: I cry aloud to the LORD; I lift up my voice for mercy. Consider asking God about the cries you've muffled — what needs shouting that you've only whispered.
